items.q: How do you say 'I use my phone every day' in German?
items.correct: Ich benutze mein Handy jeden Tag.
items.misconception: Ich nutze mein Telefon jeden Tag.
items.random_wrong: Ich gebrauche mein Phone jeden Tag.
items.why: Students use 'Telefon' (which means landline/telephone). 'Handy' is the German word for mobile phone - confusingly looks like an English adjective but is the standard term. Also: BENUTZEN is the most common 'to use'.

items.q: How does 'der Mann' change in the accusative case?
items.why: Students think English-style: objects are unchanged. German changes ARTICLES based on case. Masculine is the only gender that visibly changes in accusative: der to den. Feminine, neuter, and plural look the same.

items.q: Which relative pronoun do you use for 'the man WHOM I saw' in German?
items.why: Students don't decline relative pronouns. The relative pronoun takes the case its FUNCTION REQUIRES in its clause. 'Whom I saw' makes it the direct object, so accusative: 'Der Mann, DEN ich gesehen habe.'

items.q: When did the DSGVO come into force?
items.why: Correct answer: May 2018. “May 2016” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Illegal online content. “Music piracy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is the maximum NetzDG fine in millions of euros?
items.why: The real figure is 50. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
